items.q: What is the cosmic microwave background?
items.correct: Relic radiation from when the universe became transparent, about 380,000 years after the Big Bang.
items.misconception: Background noise from human microwave ovens.
items.random_wrong: Light from the most distant galaxies.
items.why: Students take 'microwave' as the appliance. The CMB is photons from the early universe, now stretched to microwave wavelengths by expansion - the earliest light we can detect, predicted by Big Bang theory.

items.q: What is the current best estimate for the age of the universe?
items.why: Students may hold non-scientific views or guess. The current consensus from CMB measurements and stellar ages is approximately 13.8 billion years - the time since the Big Bang.

items.q: What is a black hole's event horizon?
items.correct: The boundary beyond which not even light can escape.
items.misconception: The visible 'edge' of the black hole.
items.random_wrong: A literal horizon you can walk to.
items.why: Students picture a surface. The event horizon is a mathematical BOUNDARY - nothing is physically there. Once inside, escape requires faster-than-light speed, which is impossible.
It's 2.7. “30” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Hydrogen and helium. “Iron and silicon” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the CMB discovered?
items.why: Exact answer: 1964. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
